query: current research on obesity and wheezing in adolescents

retrieved_docs.title: Obesity Effects on Child Health(Archived) -- Clinical Significance -- Asthma
retrieved_docs.content: Overweight or obese children have been observed to have a higher prevalence of asthma and asthma exacerbations. The link between asthma and obesity is mediated through abnormal inflammatory and oxidant stress, chest restriction with airway narrowing, and obesity-related comorbidities such as obstructive sleep apnea and gastroesophageal reflux. [30]

retrieved_docs.title: Obesity in Pediatric Patients -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Pulmonary comorbidities include asthma and OSA. Children with asthma may report shortness of breath, cough, and exercise intolerance, while those with OSA may exhibit symptoms such as snoring, restlessness, daytime sleepiness, and behavioral problems. Pulmonary function tests and polysomnography may be warranted for further evaluation.

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Asthma -- Etiology -- Infancy, Childhood, and Adolescence
retrieved_docs.content: Viral respiratory tract infections during infancy, especially those caused by respiratory syncytial virus and human rhinovirus, are predictors of subsequent asthma development in childhood and adulthood. In addition, it remains uncertain if the specific infections directly cause asthma or if wheezing during the infections predicts future asthma development. Additionally, early-life exposure to air pollution, such as products of combustion from gas-fired appliances and indoor fires, obesity, and early puberty, also increases the risk of developing asthma.

retrieved_docs.title: Obesity in Pediatric Patients -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Clinicians should also assess for dyspnea or wheezing, which may indicate asthma, and for abdominal tenderness or hepatomegaly, which may indicate MASLD. Limited hip or knee mobility may be a sign of SCFE and Blount disease, respectively.

retrieved_docs.title: Asthma -- Etiology -- Childhood
retrieved_docs.content: Wheezing caused by viral infections, particularly respiratory syncytial virus and human rhinovirus, may predispose infants and young children to develop asthma later in life. In addition, early-life exposure to air pollution, including combustion by-products from gas-fired appliances and indoor fires, obesity, and early puberty, also increases the risk of asthma.

retrieved_docs.title: Obesity Effects on Child Health(Archived) -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Childhood obesity significantly impacts both physical and psychological health. Obesity can lead to severe health conditions, including non-insulin-dependent diabetes, cardiovascular problems, bronchial asthma, obstructive sleep apnea (OSA), hypertension, hepatic steatosis, gastroesophageal reflux (GER), and psychosocial issues. The preventive and therapeutic interventions in childhood obesity are crucial in decreasing the burden of comorbid health conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: Obesity Effects on Child Health(Archived) -- Clinical Significance -- Academic Performance
retrieved_docs.content: Children who are obese and have comorbid health problems like diabetes, asthma, or sleep apnea miss school more frequently, thereby affecting their school performance negatively.

retrieved_docs.title: Status Asthmaticus -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Asthma constitutes a chronic inflammatory airway disease affecting millions worldwide, characterized by airway hyperresponsiveness, bronchoconstriction, mucus hypersecretion, and airway remodeling. Status asthmaticus comprises a severe, life-threatening exacerbation marked by persistent bronchospasm unresponsive to standard bronchodilators and corticosteroids. Common triggers include viral respiratory infections, allergen exposure, medication noncompliance, and environmental pollutants. Risk factors for status asthmaticus include poorly controlled asthma, previous severe exacerbations, and comorbidities such as obesity or chronic rhinosinusitis.

retrieved_docs.title: Wheezing(Archived) -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: The reported prevalence of wheezing amongst young children between 2 to 3 years of age is 26% in the United States. The global prevalence is lower in the adolescent age range, approximately 12%. Global surveys reveal a similar prevalence in adults, with European and Australian surveys reporting the highest prevalence rates, up to 17%. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Obesity in Pediatric Patients -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Other studies may also be considered when clinically indicated. Imaging modalities include radiographs of the hip and knee joints to look for signs of SCFE and Blount disease, as well as ultrasound of the abdomen and pelvis to search for features consistent with PCOS. Brain computed tomography or magnetic resonance imaging may be used to assess for hypothalamic tumors. Polysomnography is used to confirm or exclude OSA, while pulmonary function testing assists in diagnosing asthma. A lumbar puncture with measurement of cerebrospinal fluid opening pressure may be performed to evaluate for pseudotumor cerebri.

retrieved_docs.title: Obesity in Pediatric Patients -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The 2025 Global Commission, an expert panel convened by The Lancet Diabetes and Endocrinology and composed of approximately 56 leading specialists and patient representatives, published a report identifying 13 diagnostic criteria that indicate obesity-related organ dysfunction. The included clinical features are as follows: Symptoms of increased intracranial pressure, including vision loss and recurrent headaches OSA Breathlessness or wheezing attributable to excess weight Hypertension Presence of hyperglycemia, hypertriglyceridemia, and low high-density lipoprotein cholesterol (HDL-C) Elevated liver enzymes consistent with MASLD Microalbuminuria Recurrent urinary incontinence PCOS Recurrent pain, tripping, or falling due to pes planus or lower-limb malalignment Recurrent pain or reduced mobility associated with tibia vara SCFE Limitations in day-to-day function or mobility

retrieved_docs.title: Obesity in Pediatric Patients -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Diabetes: Polyuria and polydipsia Pseudotumor cerebri: Headache and vision changes Blount disease and slipped capital femoral epiphysis (SCFE): Limp with diminished internal rotation of the hip and associated hip or knee pain Gallbladder disease and metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease (MASLD): Abdominal pain, vomiting, and jaundice Behavioral or psychological problems: Anxiety and depression, worsening school performance, and experiences of bullying Asthma and obstructive sleep apnea (OSA): Shortness of breath, snoring, and daytime sleepiness or fatigue Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS): Irregular menses, hirsutism, and acne

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Asthma -- Etiology -- Infancy, Childhood, and Adolescence
retrieved_docs.content: Risk factors during infancy and childhood include male sex until age 20 when the incidence equalizes, abnormal neonatal lung function, atopy, sensitization and exposure to common allergens, obesity, and early puberty. [18] Some studies indicate that the microbiome may also play a role. Exposure to certain bacteria and common allergens within the first year of life may lower the incidence of asthma, whereas exposure later increases the risk. [19]

retrieved_docs.content: Loud, habitual snoring with witnessed breathing pauses Excessive daytime sleepiness and fatigue Morning headaches and dry mouth Difficulty concentrating and irritability In children: behavioral problems, poor school performance, enuresis

retrieved_docs.title: Asthma Medication in Children -- Issues of Concern -- Comorbidities
retrieved_docs.content: Comorbidities such as chronic rhinosinusitis, obesity, gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD), and obstructive sleep apnea are associated with flare-ups and poor quality of life. Treatment of comorbid conditions is essential; however, management can complicate treatment adherence and asthma control due to polypharmacy. [52]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 1 -- Community 166
retrieved_docs.content: a repository for miscellaneous respiratory interventions. Collectively, these entities form a network of diagnostic and therapeutic actions centered on the larynx, trachea, bronchi, and lung within the thoracic cavity, organized through parent‑child hierarchies, broader procedural groupings, and anatomical associations that are essential for coordinated patient care in respiratory and thoracic medicine.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1952
retrieved_docs.content: Infant preterm low birth weight is clinically associated with both obesity (الرَبْو) and short stature (قصر القامة). Obesity shares clinical similarity with asthma and is linked to an increased risk of neonatal death (وفاة حديث الولادة). Environmental exposure to air pollution (znečištění ovzduší) is related to obesity, and tobacco use (تعاطي التَبغ) is also clinically associated with obesity. Short stature is likewise associated with neonatal death. These interconnections illustrate how low birth weight, growth abnormalities, respiratory conditions, environmental factors, and lifestyle behaviors converge to affect infant mortality and long‑term health outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2257
retrieved_docs.content: INFANT PRETERM LOW BIRTH WEIGHT is clinically associated with الرَبْو (cough), indicating that premature infants with low birth weight frequently exhibit cough as a respiratory manifestation. Asthma is clinically similar to الرَبْو, reflecting that the cough experienced in asthma shares comparable characteristics and underlying airway inflammation. Additionally, الرَبْو is related to znečištění ovzduší (air pollution), highlighting that exposure to polluted air can trigger or worsen cough. Together, these relationships underscore the importance of monitoring cough in vulnerable populations such as preterm low‑birth‑weight infants and asthmatic patients, and of addressing environmental air quality to reduce respiratory symptoms and improve patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2937
retrieved_docs.content: The term “has parent” is classified as a not otherwise specified (NOS) entity within the broader category of other upper respiratory disease, indicating that it represents an unspecified or generic parent relationship in the context of upper respiratory conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 115
retrieved_docs.content: fetal growth and malnutrition” category, diabetes mellitus (مرض السكري), cesarean delivery, obesity (الرَبْو) and neonatal death (وفاة حديث الولادة). Respiratory distress syndrome from immature lung development is a common etiologic factor for preterm birth, and asthma is noted as clinically similar to obesity. Obesity is closely connected to diabetes and is influenced by environmental air‑pollution exposure (znečištění ovzduší); inhaled corticosteroids—beclomethasone dipropionate monohydrate, beklomethason dipropionát and flutikason propionát—may prevent obesity, while the mast‑cell stabilizer disodium cromoglycate is contraindicated in obese individuals. A parent diagnostic component “ONDORII BIGARRENDARRAK (EPA LUZERA) SINTESI, PROTESE MATERIA‑LA, BIKG” groups disease elements such as an unspecified artificial body opening and the endocrine, metabolic and nutritional disease category (Anden endokr/metab/enaeringssygdom), serving as a higher‑level parent for the Diagnosis/Diseases

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1588
retrieved_docs.content: Other and unspecified environmental and accidental causes is classified as a child category under the broader parent grouping “e codes: other specified; nec,” indicating that this specific set of environmental and accidental etiologies is organized within the external cause (E‑code) taxonomy as an “other specified; not elsewhere classified” subgroup.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1723
retrieved_docs.content: The term خديج designates infants who have completed 29–30 weeks of gestation, and this developmental stage is directly linked to the occurrence of متلازمة خلل نضج الرئة (pulmonary immaturity syndrome). Consequently, babies classified as خديج are at risk for respiratory complications arising from lung immaturity, highlighting the importance of recognizing this gestational age category when assessing and managing neonatal respiratory health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 4 -- Community 2793
retrieved_docs.content: Apnea (الاِختِنَاق) is the overarching respiratory disturbance that encompasses the broader category of respiratory disorder (اضطراب تَنَفُّسِيّ). Within this category, the clinical condition described as respiratory failure; insufficiency; arrest (adult) represents a more severe manifestation. Thus, adult respiratory failure, insufficiency, and arrest are subtypes of the respiratory disorder, which itself is nested under the primary concept of apnea, illustrating a hierarchical relationship that links a fundamental breathing abnormality to increasingly critical respiratory pathologies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1826
retrieved_docs.content: The category “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions” is presented as a parent concept that includes the specific child entity “غشي.” In this framework, the general term “Symptoms” is described as closely related to, and potentially synonymous with, the broader phrase “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions,” indicating that the two expressions refer to overlapping clinical manifestations. Consequently, the specific manifestation “غشي” is understood to fall under the umbrella of nonspecific or ill‑defined symptomatology, and clinicians should recognize its inclusion within this broader symptom category when evaluating and documenting patient presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2902
retrieved_docs.content: Certain conditions originating in the perinatal period (P00‑P96) represent a broad category of early‑life health problems, and within this group the respiratory and cardiovascular disorders specific to the perinatal period (P19‑P29) are classified as a child subgroup. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1701
retrieved_docs.content: Respiratory failure; insufficiency; arrest (adult) is a specific adult respiratory condition that is classified under the broader category of اضطراب تَنَفُّسِيّ (respiratory disorder).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2367
retrieved_docs.content: The term “nos” is classified as a child of “other upper respiratory disease,” and “other upper respiratory disease” in turn is a child of the broader category “اضطرابات أنفية، غير مصنف في موضع آخر” (nasal disorders, not classified elsewhere). The clinical procedure “control of epistaxis” is performed directly at the site identified as “nos” and its primary focus is “الرُعاف” (epistaxis). Additionally, “الرُعاف” is recognized as a child condition within the overarching group “اضطرابات أنفية، غير مصنف في موضع آخر.” This hierarchy links the nonspecific upper respiratory condition (“nos”) to a specific nasal disorder category and highlights that the management of epistaxis targets the “nos” site while addressing the condition “الرُعاف,” which itself belongs to the same broader nasal disorder classification.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1771
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms are closely related to and may be considered synonymous with the broader category of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions. In this hierarchical framework, diseases and injuries are classified as having the parent category of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, indicating that they are understood through the manifestation of such nonspecific signs. A specific example of a child element within this category is الانهيار في الدورة الدموية (circulatory collapse), which represents a distinct clinical manifestation that can arise from ill‑defined conditions. Together, these relationships highlight that the identification of symptoms and signs, including serious events like circulatory collapse, is essential for recognizing and managing diseases and injuries that present without clear, well‑defined diagnostic criteria.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 857
retrieved_docs.content: The category “عملية في المجرى التنفسي” (procedure in the respiratory tract) serves as a parent classification for several specific interventions. Under this parent, “Diagnostic bronchoscopy and biopsy of bronchus” is identified as a distinct diagnostic procedure, as is “Other diagnostic procedures of respiratory tract and mediastinum,” which groups additional diagnostic techniques involving the airway and mediastinum. In addition to these diagnostic actions, the therapeutic set “الإجراءات العلاجية الخاصة بالسبيل التنفسي، غير مصنف في موضع آخر” (therapeutic procedures specific to the respiratory tract, not classified elsewhere) is also listed as a child of the same parent category. This hierarchical organization groups both diagnostic and therapeutic respiratory procedures under a common umbrella, facilitating clear classification, documentation, and management of airway‑related medical interventions.
